content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

October 28, 1991

Loystene L. Irvin
Board of Education - Seat 5
Inglewood Unified School District
10236 - 2nd Avenue
Inglewood, CA 90303

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-446

Dear Ms. Irvin:

This is in response to your letter requesting guidance in
connection with the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 You have
asked several questions of a general nature; we are, therefore,
treating your letter as a request for informal assistance.2

QUESTIONS

1. May campaign funds be used to pay off outstanding loans
of a campaign committee?

2. May personal funds be used to pay off outstanding loans
of a campaign committee?

3. May a portion of a loan or an entire loan to a campaign
committee be forgiven by the lender?

4. Must a penalty be paid in connection with forgiveness of
a loan?

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I 91-446
Page No. 2


5.  May campaign contributions be solicited after the
election for which the campaign was waged?

CONCLUSIONS

1.  Campaign funds may be used to pay off outstanding loans
of a campaign committee.

2.  Personal funds of a candidate may not be used to pay
outstanding campaign committee loans unless the funds have been
deposited in the campaign bank account prior to using them for
loan payments.

3.  There is nothing prohibiting forgiveness of a loan or
any portion of a loan to a campaign committee. However,
forgiveness of a loan would be considered to be a contribution in
the amount forgiven, and must be reported as such.

4.  There is no penalty imposed by the Act for forgiveness
of a loan.

5.  Contributions may be solicited after the election for
which the campaign committee was formed.

FACTS

Your letter reveals few facts pertaining to the questions
posed therein. In a recent telephone conversation with you
indicated that in April, 1991 you ran for a seat on your local
Board of Education. Since no candidate in the April election
received the required 51% of the vote, you participated in a
runoff election in June, 1991, in which you won the Board of
Education seat. You have loaned your personal funds to your
campaign and others have loaned funds as well. You are concerned
about repayment of those loans from campaign contributions.

ANALYSIS

Nothing in the Political Reform Act prohibits candidates from
accepting loans subject to repayment. Section 84216 provides that
a loan received by a candidate or a committee is a contribution
and should be reported. Thus, the Act recognizes that candidates
and committees do accept loans.

The Act also regulates how campaign funds may be used.
Sections 89510-89521. An expenditure of campaign funds, including
an expenditure to repay a loan to a campaign committee, must be
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
Where campaign funds are used to repay a loan made to a campaign,
the repayment will be considered to be directly related to a
political purpose. Therefore, it is permissible to use campaign
funds to repay a loan to your campaign committee.

Our File No. I 91-446
Page No. 3


In connection with your second question, you may use personal
funds to repay a loan to your campaign, but you must first deposit
the funds into your campaign bank account. Since the loan
repayment is a campaign expenditure, and since, pursuant to
Section 8502(c), all campaign expenditures must be made from a
campaign account, the funds for the loan repayment must be
deposited into a campaign account before making the loan
repayment. There is no prohibition in the Act pertaining to a
candidate's use of his or her personal funds to repay loans, so
long as the payment comes from a campaign account of the
candidate. (Lockwood Advice Letter, I-90-456.)³

There is no prohibition in the Act as to forgiveness of a
loan to a campaign committee. However, where failure to collect
on a loan is intended to bestow a political benefit, forgiveness
of the loan will be considered a contribution, and the
contribution would be subject to appropriate reporting. Where a
lender determines that a loan is not collectable as a result of a
bona fide business judgment and reasonable efforts to collect on
the loan have not been successful, the amount forgiven will not be
deemed to be a contribution. (Soloman Advice Letter, A-91-246.)
There is no penalty connected with forgiveness per se; however, if
a forgiven loan is not correctly reported as a contribution, that
could be a violation of the Act which could result in imposition
of a fine on the violator.

Your final question concerns the permissibility of soliciting
funds after the date of the election for which the soliciting
campaign committee was formed. There is no prohibition in the
Political Reform Act that would prevent you from raising
additional campaign funds to pay debts incurred in running your
elective office. Funds received for this purpose would be
contributive and must be reported in the same manner as any other
contribution. We suggest, however, that you inquire as to whether
there are any local restrictions on campaign fund solicitation
after an election has occurred; since the jurisdiction of the Fair
Political Practices Commission is limited to the Political Reform
Act, we are unable to advise on local campaign finance rules.


³ Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
many Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

Our File No. I 91-446
Page No. 4


     I trust that the above answers your questions.  If you have
need of further information, please do not hesitate to contact me
at (916) 322-5901.
                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [illegible]
                                        Susan L. Borrow
                                        Counsel, Legal Division


SH:SLB

sections.question:
1. May campaign funds be used to pay off outstanding loans of a campaign committee?

2. May personal funds be used to pay off outstanding loans of a campaign committee?

3. May a portion of a loan or an entire loan to a campaign committee be forgiven by the lender?

4. Must a penalty be paid in connection with forgiveness of a loan?

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18329(c)(3).)

5. May campaign contributions be solicited after the election for which the campaign was waged?
sections.conclusion:
1. Campaign funds may be used to pay off outstanding loans of a campaign committee.

2. Personal funds of a candidate may not be used to pay outstanding campaign committee loans unless the funds have been deposited in the campaign bank account prior to using them for loan payments.

3. There is nothing prohibiting forgiveness of a loan or any portion of a loan to a campaign committee. However, forgiveness of a loan would be considered to be a contribution in the amount forgiven, and must be reported as such.

4. There is no penalty imposed by the Act for forgiveness of a loan.

5. Contributions may be solicited after the election for which the campaign committee was formed.
sections.facts: Your letter reveals few facts pertaining to the questions posed therein. In a recent telephone conversation with me you indicated that in April, 1991 you ran for a seat on your local Board of Education. Since no candidate in the April election received the required 51% of the vote, you participated in a runoff election in June, 1991, in which you won the Board of Education seat. You have loaned your personal funds to your campaign and others have loaned funds as well. You are concerned about repayment of those loans from campaign contributions.
sections.analysis:
Nothing in the Political Reform Act prohibits candidates from accepting loans subject to repayment. Section 84216 provides that a loan received by a candidate or a committee is a contribution and should be reported. Thus, the Act recognizes that candidates and committees do accept loans.

The Act also regulates how campaign funds may be used. Sections 89510-89521. An expenditure of campaign funds, including an expenditure to repay a loan to a campaign committee, must be related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. Where campaign funds are used to repay a loan made to a campaign, the repayment will be considered to be directly related to a political purpose. Therefore, it is permissible to use campaign funds to repay a loan to your campaign committee.

In connection with your second question, you may use personal funds to repay a loan to your campaign, but you must first deposit the funds into your campaign bank account. Since the loan repayment is a campaign expenditure, and since, pursuant to Section 85201(e), all campaign expenditures must be made from a campaign account, the funds for the loan repayment must be deposited into a campaign account before making the loan repayment. There is no prohibition in the Act pertaining to a candidate's use of his or her personal funds to repay loans, so long as the payment comes from a campaign account of the candidate. (Lockwood Advice Letter, I-90-456.)3

There is no prohibition in the Act as to forgiveness of a loan to a campaign committee. However, where failure to collect on a loan is intended to bestow a political benefit, forgiveness of the loan will be considered a contribution, and the contribution would be subject to appropriate reporting. Where a lender determines that a loan is not collectable as a result of a bona fide business judgment and reasonable efforts to collect on the loan have not been successful, the amount forgiven will not be deemed to be a contribution. (Soloman Advice Letter, A-91-246.) There is no penalty connected with forgiveness per se; however, if a forgiven loan is not correctly reported as a contribution, that could be a violation of the Act which could result in imposition of a fine on the violator.

Your final question concerns the permissibility of soliciting funds after the date of the election for which the soliciting campaign committee was formed. There is no prohibition in the Political Reform Act that would prevent you from raising additional campaign funds to pay debts incurred in running for your elective office. Funds received for this purpose would be contributions and must be reported in the same manner as any other contribution. We suggest, however, that you inquire as to whether there are any local restrictions on campaign fund solicitation after an election has occurred; since the jurisdiction of the Fair Political Practices Commission is limited to the Political Reform Act, we are unable to advise on local campaign finance rules.

3 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a cost of 10¢ per page.

I trust that the above answers your questions. If you have need of further information, please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

4. Must a penalty be paid in connection with forgiveness of a loan?

CONCLUSION: 1. Campaign funds may be used to pay off outstanding loans of a campaign committee.

2. Personal funds of a candidate may not be used to pay outstanding campaign committee loans unless the funds have been deposited in the campaign bank account prior to using them for loan payments.

3. There is nothing prohibiting forgiveness of a loan or any portion of a loan to a campaign committee. However, forgiveness of a loan would be considered to be a contribution in the amount forgiven, and must be reported as such.

4. There is no penalty imposed by the Act for forgiveness of a loan.

5. Contributions may be solicited after the election for which the campaign committee was formed.
